entry.title: SAP Fiori Tools 2603 Update: RAP Project Accelerator, Walkthrough, Adaptation Improvements, and More
entry.content:
With SAP Fiori tools 2603, we deliver a set of focused improvements across development transparency, onboarding, AI-supported development, and extensibility. Highlights include Project Accelerator support for RAP, a change in navigation from properties to its source, a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code, and the ability to import existing key user changes into an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project.

Project Accelerator for RAP generates full-stack SAP Fiori elements applications from text or images

One of the most important additions in this release is the extension of the Project Accelerator to the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). The Project Accelerator has been available for SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) scenarios since 2024 and supports rapid prototyping with AI, based on images and/or a requirements description. With release 2603, it can now also create ABAP artifacts and services with AI assistance, addressing a long-standing request.

This significantly broadens the reach of the Project Accelerator for enterprise SAP development. The feature is available for cloud-based RAP back ends with SAP BTP or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 or higher. For more information please refer to the SAP Help Portal page Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI. A separate blog post dedicated to this addition is currently in preparation and will be linked here once published.

The Project Accelerator lets you generate applications directly from business requirements documents, either text or images.

 

 

 

 

The Project Accelerator for RAP builds on the capabilities already available for CAP.

 

Direct navigation to source in the manifest gives developers a more transparent editing experience

Since the beginning, SAP Fiori tools has followed a clear principle: support development tasks with convenience, but do not hide the underlying technical details from experienced developers. This is why properties and settings in the tooling generally allow navigation to the corresponding source. For annotations and XML-based settings, this already opens the relevant annotation or fragment file directly. For manifest-related changes, however, SAP Fiori tools previously used a simplified virtual representation of the manifest as an intermediate editing layer.

Over time, this abstraction became more of a liability than a benefit. It introduced additional complexity, made parts of the implementation more error-prone, and did not provide enough added value compared to working directly with the actual manifest.json. With release 2603, source links for manifest properties now open the corresponding location in the real manifest and highlight the relevant section directly. This gives developers a more transparent editing experience and simplifies the path from visual tooling to source code. It also creates room for future optimizations in areas such as undo/redo handling and patch logic, which are planned for a later release.

New walkthrough for getting started makes novice developers more productive more quickly

Users who already work with SAP Fiori tools often appreciate the convenience and breadth of what the tooling offers. At the same time, the number of available capabilities can make it harder for newcomers to find a good starting point. To make onboarding easier, we have added a new walkthrough in Visual Studio Code that helps users get started with SAP Fiori tools directly inside the IDE.

The walkthrough provides a practical entry point into the most relevant concepts and flows. It guides users through the initial setup and encourages hands-on exploration of the tooling. If you are new to SAP Fiori tools, or if you want a structured way to revisit the basics, try it out and complete the tasks.

The walkthrough can be started from the Help menu in VS Code, choose Open Walkthrough… and then SAP Fiori tools from the drop-down menu.

The SAP Fiori tools walkthrough explains all the capabilities and has hands-on opportunities to explore and learn.

Import key user changes into an Adaptation Project and keep your edits

With this release, when creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project, you can now import existing key user changes. This helps ensure that changes made through key user adaptation are not lost when moving to developer extensibility. The feature is available starting with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

For more background, read about key user adaptation and the transition to developer extensibility.

Additional new features in SAP Fiori tools 2603 and more planned

Release 2603 includes an update of the tools and generator to support ESLint v9. In parallel, we are internally testing a new validation feature that allows more flexible, domain-specific rules and visual integration of fix options into the Page Map and Page Editor. We plan to release this together with an initial ruleset in one of the upcoming SAP Fiori tools releases and cover it in a separate blog post.

There is also an improved support for value help services referenced by an app’s main OData V4 service. Their metadata can now be retrieved during app generation, which enables SAP Fiori tools to support scenarios such as property configuration for these value helps and mock data creation for preview. This work is part of a broader rework of test generation support that we plan to cover in a future release blog.

The MCP server for SAP Fiori can now generate an SAP Fiori elements application for an existing OData service, including services exposed from a RAP back end. It uses saved system connections from the Connection Manager extension together with a system-relative service URI. This means that the authentication flow can remain outside the AI client and the LLM, following the established SAP system sign-in flow. This is one step toward end-to-end, AI-driven, RAP-based application development in Visual Studio Code, and we will continue to expand this path in upcoming releases.

If you want to stay up to date with changes in SAP Fiori tools between release blog posts, the What’s New section in the SAP Help Portal remains the best place to track newly delivered capabilities and fixes.

The SAP Fiori development portal includes explanations and sample code for all the building blocks available for creating SAP Fiori elements applications.

entry.title: AI and UX sessions at SAP Sapphire 2026 that I’m most excited about
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire is where SAP makes its big announcements about its vision for the future of ERP and business applications. This includes showing new products, presenting customers who have transformed their business using SAP’s solutions, and highlighting new technologies that will shape the future.

For 2026, AI will continue to dominate the event. AI will change the way that people consume enterprise software through chat-based interfaces and screens that generate on-the-fly based on what you want to do. Eventually.

In this post, I’ll share the sessions at Sapphire and ASUG Annual Conference that I’m most excited about. For the full menu, you can browse the session catalog. My preferences are, of course, shaped by my role on the Cloud ERP UX Foundation team. I’ll organize by date so you can plan your agenda. You can also use the browser to search by topic or speaker if that makes it easier for you. Some are even available on-demand. Let’s go!

Monday, May 11, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Monday
10am – 5pm
ASUG102

Unlocking Business Value with AI: Strategy, Security, and the Road Ahead

Gain a comprehensive and practical overview of SAP Business AI. This workshop will examine core strategies, security frameworks, and future trends while exploring real-world customer stories. The session will conclude with an expert panel and a hands-on AI road mapping session.

Many experts from SAP

Monday
2:00pm – 2:20pm
ERP2143

The future of enterprise: Innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private

Discover how SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions enable the next era of enterprise transformation. This introductory session explores how cutting-edge artificial intelligence, trusted data, and integrated applications work in harmony, creating smarter, faster, and more resilient businesses within the security and flexibility of your private cloud.

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Vinay V, SAP

Monday
2:30pm – 2:50pm
CXP2149

Legacy to legendary: The AI-enhanced path

Explore the strategic road map for moving from legacy on-premises systems to a cloud-native, AI-enhanced architecture. Discover how a clean core approach helps reduce technical debt, accelerate innovation, and position your organization to use AI for greater efficiency and agility.

 

Monday
3:30pm – 3:50pm
PUX1349

Innovations and value that SAP Business AI offers

Learn how you can find the best AI features for your use cases, and understand the business value and associated cost with our self-service tools.

Pascal Mauge, SAP

 

Tuesday, May 12, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Tuesday
10:30 am - 11:15 am PUX1228

Creating better user experiences with AI-assisted SAP Fiori apps | PUX1228

New AI features in SAP Fiori apps boost user productivity and speed onboarding. Starting with the “My Home” page and drilling down into typical SAP Fiori apps, see how AI makes it easier to find information, enter data, automate tasks, and make informed business decisions. Explore how conversational interactions make SAP solutions more intuitive.

 

Peter Spielvogel, SAP

Tuesday
11:00 am - 11:20 am PUX1346

Great features don’t guarantee success: Experience does

Explore SAP Business Suite in the cloud to discover how you can shape the experience of the product for stellar adoption success.

Daniel Becker, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm FOU1861

ASUG Community Conversation: Artificial intelligence

Explore platform strategy, embedded and generative AI use cases, integration patterns, and road map priorities with experts in AI and SAP Business Technology Platform. Discuss architecture, data readiness, and clean core alignment while exchanging lessons learned with peers advancing AI innovation.

John Astill, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm ERP1622

SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA: What’s new, why change, and why now?

 Learn from more than 2,400 cloud ERP projects as we navigate the shift from SAP ERP Central Component (SAP ECC) to SAP S/4HANA. Compare greenfield, brownfield, and selective data transitions to find the right path. Discover the key differences in SAP S/4HANA, the value of moving now, and how to accelerate AI adoption with modern technology.

 

Esther Vrieze, SAP

Bert Schulze, SAP

Tuesday
11:30 am - 12:15 pm BTM2345

Why ERP transformations fail: The change management gap

Organizations expect operational efficiency and digital capabilities from their SAP solution transformations, yet some ERP programs fail to realize full value. The missing link is often adoption. See how integrating organizational change management early in the SAP lifecycle reduces resistance, accelerates adoption, and improves business outcomes.

 

Kara Beck, Moog Inc.

Tuesday
1:00 pm - 1:45 pm ERP1864

ASUG Community Conversation: Cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA

In this interactive ASUG Community Conversation for professionals in cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA, explore strategies across migration, operation, and innovation—featuring our road maps, clean core and AI insights, and customer lessons learned. Exchange practical guidance with peers and shape future discussions through open dialogue.

Peter Spielvogel, SAP

Nicole Smythe, SAP

Tuesday
1:00 pm - 1:20 pm JOU2645

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

Unlock real value from agentic AI with insights from SAP experts and peers. Explore what transformation means today and how you can scale AI across the business, overcome common challenges, govern risk, and build an AI-first enterprise that drives measurable top- and bottom-line impact.

 

Tuesday
2:00 pm - 2:45 pm ERP1178

2026 and beyond: Discover the latest innovations in SAP Cloud ERP Private 

Running your ERP in the cloud allows you to benefit from innovations at your own pace. Explore recent AI and other functional developments planned for the next releases of SAP Cloud ERP Private solutions to help you prepare for adoption, build your road map, and accelerate time to value.

Maura Hameroff, SAP

Rudolf Hois, SAP

Tuesday
3:00 pm - 3:45 pm FOU1559

Build enterprise-grade pro-code agents on SAP Business Technology Platform

Unlock the power of autonomous agents with enterprise-grade guarantees. Get technical tips on building pro-code agents on SAP Business Technology Platform that balance innovation with responsibility. Learn to use the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for smooth tool integration while supporting security, ethical AI behavior, and regulatory compliance.

Evgenii Skrebtcov, SAP

Tim Back, SAP

Tuesday
3:00 pm - 3:45 pm ERP1834

Extend SAP S/4HANA Cloud while keeping a clean core | ERP1834

Extend the capabilities of SAP S/4HANA Cloud to address your business needs. Use AI to generate projects in the ABAP programming language and SAP Cloud Application Programming Model from your business requirements. Build SAP Fiori apps based on your written or graphical description, and use natural language filtering to find information in them.

Katja Zoch, SAP

Tuesday
3:30 pm - 3:50 pm JOU2646

AI as the new UI

The interface is disappearing, and that's the point. As AI becomes the primary way people interact with enterprise software, the question is no longer how to design better screens but how to deliver better outcomes. Intent replaces navigation, conversation replaces configuration, and the most powerful user experience is the one you barely notice.

 

Tuesday
4:00 pm - 4:20 pm PUX1192

SAP's user experience strategy for an AI-powered web and mobile future

Get an overview of how SAP is helping business users get their work done more easily and with better outcomes: using the Joule solution and embedded AI to take UX to the next level, providing a consistent and integrated suite experience, and leveraging our user research framework to meet users' needs. Also see how you can create great UX yourself.

Arin Bhowmick, SAP

 

Wednesday, May 13, 2026

Day / Time / Session ID

Session title and description

Speakers

Wednesday
10:30 am – 11:15 am SAP1623

SAP ECC to SAP Business Suite: Migration strategies and cloud insights

Master the direct transition from SAP ERP Central Component to SAP Business Suite using proven best practices. Compare public, private, and on-premises deployments to define key decision drivers. Learn to navigate annual releases, leverage essential migration tools, and identify the evolving skills needed for a successful, seamless cloud journey.

Esther Vrieze, SAP

Bert Schultze, SAP

Wednesday
10:30 am – 11:15 am PUX1819

SAP Fiori in action: How modern UX drives efficiency in cloud ERP

Get an overview of how the SAP Fiori user experience (UX) helps users get their work done more easily and efficiently. See how we are using AI today in applications and with the Joule solution. Understand our road map for revolutionizing UX, how AI changes the idea of what an application is, and what all this means for you.

Thomas Reiss, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 11:50 am SUS1523

AI that helps you operate your business

As sustainability shifts from reporting to real business decisions, it becomes a control and execution challenge. Explore how you can replace manual processes with AI for trusted, scalable insights and decision support, delivering accountability for operational and financial decision-making.

Sophia Mendelsohn, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm PUX1195

Creating AI-powered UX for your own applications with SAP Design System

Learn how to create AI-powered UX for your applications by using SAP Design System (SAP Fiori UX for web and mobile apps) with its powerful set of reusable components, patterns, and floorplans. See how building applications is made easier with our AI-enabled UI technologies for web and mobile apps.

Sophia Levens, SAP

Frederic Berg, SAP

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm ERP2238

Western Sugar’s success with SAP Cloud ERP

Five years after go-live, Western Sugar Cooperative calls SAP Cloud ERP applications “wonderful.” Hear about why it chose public cloud; the value of clean core, best practices, strong configuration and integration, and continuous improvement; its smooth move from a two- to three-system landscape; and readiness for AI-enabled innovation.

 

Wednesday
11:30 am – 12:15 pm FOU2337

When AI becomes the interface to the enterprise

For decades, SAP helped shape enterprise software based on screens and transactions. Today, AI is evolving how we interact with systems. Explore how SAP leads this shift—uniting applications, data, and AI—and what this next phase of transformation means for organizations turning AI into a real competitive advantage.

Florian Heretsch, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm FOU1557

Getting started with SAP Build MCPs: Create your first CAP app and process

 Experience the powerful Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration in SAP Build solutions. Produce your first SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) application and SAP Fiori elements UI, while orchestrating workflows. See how AI-enhanced context improves coding efficiency and helps you build scalable, process-rich apps.

 

Tim Back, SAP

Jason Gabor, SAP

Wednesday
1:00 pm – 1:45 pm BTM1232

The AI-augmented architect: Upgrading architecture in the age of AI 

 AI is reshaping enterprise architecture. To provide value, enterprise architectures must embrace AI tools. Discover SAP technology leveraging AI for enhanced visibility and coordination. Through real-world examples, see how Joule and agentic AI increase information velocity and augment architect capabilities to thrive in this evolving landscape.

Martin Mysyk, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm PUX1210

AI and the future of user experience in SAP software

Prepare yourself for the upcoming revolution in user experience by understanding how we are harnessing AI to help users achieve better outcomes. Discover how AI is changing the idea of what an application is—and what this means for you. Learn about our AI design patterns and get a glimpse of the future SAP software user experience.

Emil Voutta, SAP

Wednesday
2:00 pm – 2:20 pm JOU2647

The future of enterprise applications and AI experiences

The enterprise application is no longer a system of record but a system of action. As AI moves from assistant to agent, organizations shift from what their software can store to what it can do. Learn why the future belongs to applications that don't just surface information but understand context, anticipate decisions, and act with confidence.

 

Wednesday
3:00 pm – 3:20 pm PUX1194

User experience innovations and road map for SAP Business Suite

Have a look at the latest user experience innovations across our suite of applications, along with exciting web and mobile innovations coming soon. Also check out selected highlights from Ul technologies for creating great UX for your own applications.

Tom Reiss, SAP

I’m excited to hear what we announce in the keynotes and experience the future of enterprise software. AI will transform how we all interact with apps. The future starts now.

Here are some other summaries of Sapphire sessions from other colleagues.

Inside SAP Build at SAP Sapphire 2026: What to Expect from AI Agents and Agentic Development by Mark Wright

See you in Orlando!

For the SAP Cloud ERP UX Foundation team, Peter Spielvogel.

entry.title: Your AI Models Just Got a Hardware Upgrade
entry.content:
Co-Author: Lukas Pätz

TL;DR

SAP AI Core (the engine) now supports flexible instance types — pick the exact CPU or GPU your workload needs
New instance types deliver up to 4.5–5x the compute performance of old resource plans
Access the latest GPUs (L4, L40S, H100) without waiting for SAP to package them into plans
Your existing resource plans still work — instance types are opt-in where they help

Start Here: SAP Note 3660109

Why This Matters

If you're running your custom AI models on SAP's Generative AI Hub (technical service SAP AI Core), this one's for you.

We've heard the feedback: newer GPUs are available from hyperscalers, but you can't use them yet because SAP hasn't released a matching resource plan. That lag is frustrating — especially when the difference between a V100 and an H100 isn't incremental. It's generational. We're talking 4.5–5x the compute performance for the right workloads. An inference call that takes 8 seconds on older hardware might finish in under 1 second on newer chips. That's the gap between "possible in theory" and "shipping in production."

Starting now, that gap is closing.
 

What Changed: Resource Plans → Instance Types

Think of the old model like a set menu at a restaurant. Curated, predictable, but limited to what the chef decided to offer.

Instance types are the shift to à la carte. You see the full range of CPUs and GPUs available across cloud providers, and you pick exactly what your workload needs — from a lightweight CPU for preprocessing to an H100 for training large models.

Resource plans aren't going away. They still work. But instance types are now the primary path for getting the newest, most capable hardware into your deployments — faster, and with finer control than the old model allowed. We highly recommend to leverage instance types going forward (only available in the "extended" service plan, now be the right time to migrate from standard to extended)

Here's the point: you tell us what your model needs; we make sure the right compute is there. Bon appétit.

What You Actually Get

A quick clarification: If you're using pre-integrated foundation models through SAP's Generative AI Hub — GPT-5.2, Claude 4.6 Opus or Gemini — this update doesn't directly affect you. Those models are already managed and optimized; you just call the API.

Instance types matter when you're running your own models: custom fine-tuned models, your proprietary models (like team-built solutions), embedding models for RAG pipelines, or specialized computer vision and industry models. That's when hardware choice becomes your concern — and when better hardware translates directly into better outcomes.

Faster inference, faster training

This isn't about spec sheets. It's about what becomes possible with the models you've built.

Custom models that were borderline viable for real-time use — domain-specific classifiers, fine-tuned embedding models, proprietary prediction models — become clearly viable when you can run them on L40S instead of T4. Training jobs that took days can finish in hours on H100. Features your team considered out of reach might now fit inside your existing budget.

L4 and L40S chips deliver substantially higher throughput than T4, at lower cost per inference call. H100 provides order-of-magnitude gains over V100 for training workloads. If you're pushing the limits of what's feasible with custom models, this matters.

Better cost efficiency — without re-architecting

Faster hardware means less compute time per job, which means lower cost. Simple as that.

Instance types also let you right-size. You stop paying for a resource plan heavier than you need. The result: better cost-per-output without touching your model or application logic.

 

New hardware when it ships — not months later

When hyperscalers release the next GPU generation, you won't be waiting for SAP to define a new resource plan. Instance types let us surface new hardware significantly faster. Your infrastructure stays current.

Multi-cloud, multi-region resilience

Instance types are available across cloud providers and regions. Deploy closer to your users or data. Meet compliance requirements. Reduce single-vendor dependency. Keep two or three validated alternatives per region and you're protected against capacity constraints.

Which Instance Should You Use?

 

WorkloadRecommended InstanceExamples
Orchestration, preprocessing, CPU-based MLCPU (AWS m7i, Azure Dv6, GCP n4)Data pipelines, API routing, lightweight model serving
Custom model inference (high throughput)L4 / L40S GPU (AWS g6, g6e)Fine-tuned classifiers, embedding models, custom vision models
Fine-tuning and training medium-to-large modelsA100 (GCP A2 Ultra)Domain-specific model fine-tuning, complex custom inference
Training large language modelsH100 (Azure NC H100 v5)Custom LLM training, proprietary foundation model development

Match the instance to your workload. Account for regional availability. Keep at least two alternatives validated per region.

Use the compute factors in SAP Note 3660109 or the SAP Cost Calculator to compare options on a consistent basis — each instance type maps to a compute factor in node-hours, which makes cost planning straightforward.

Migration: No Big Bang Required

Both options work today. You don't have to migrate everything at once. More details:

# This still works
ai.sap.com/resourcePlan: "infer.s"

# This is the new option — use where it helps
ai.sap.com/instanceType: "g6.4xlarge"

Start with the workloads where newer hardware will have the clearest impact — typically your highest-volume inference jobs or your most compute-heavy training runs. Everything else can stay on resource plans, but newer hardware might be more efficient.

Full specs, regional availability, and compute factors: SAP Note 3660109.

Staying Current

Hardware in AI is moving fast — faster than enterprise software typically keeps up. Instance types are how we keep you on the right side of that curve, without adding operational overhead to your team.

To stay informed:

  • Subscribe to SAP Note 3660109 — it's the live catalogue; new instance types show up here as they become available
  • Watch the What's New page for implementation guidance and new instance families

The Bottom Line

This isn't a small tweak. It's a deliberate shift to put more control in your hands — and to get it there faster.

We handle the complexity: evaluating new hardware, qualifying it, making it available at scale. You focus on what you're actually here to do: build AI solutions that matter.

The menu just expanded. Pick your instance — and dig in.

entry.title: April fool day riddles for LLMs
entry.content:
On this April’s Fools’ Day 2026, we tested different Ai agents with 3 riddles.

 

Riddle A for GPT-5

 

User prompt

Explain the uncommon rule that was used to write the 3 following phrases.

Phrase 1: Kids like marshmallows.

Phrase 2: After becoming clerk, Douglas E Ferguson got humbly in joy.

Phrase 3: Her inmate Janis knows literature, macrame, not opera.

 

Answer from the machine

Each phrase is an abecedarian: the initial letters of consecutive words follow the alphabet in order.

Phrase 2: After (A) becoming (B) clerk (C), Douglas (D) E (E) Ferguson (F) got (G) humbly (H) in (I) joy (J).

Phrase 3: Her (H) inmate (I) Janis (J) knows (K) literature (L), macrame (M), not (N) opera (O).

Phrase 1: Kids (K) like (L) marshmallows (M).

So the “uncommon rule” is that each successive word starts with the next letter of the alphabet.

 

 

Riddle B for Gemini 2.5 Pro

 

User prompt

The 3 dialogue excerpts below have the same unusual constraint; generate 3 new dialogue excerpts that all respect that constraint.

Dialogue excerpt 1: Pick Kevin next time!

Dialogue excerpt 2: Never regret, Tina, and don’t theorize endlessly.

Dialogue excerpt 3: Yes. Suddenly you understand Diego’s situation.

 

Answer from the machine

The unusual constraint is that the last letter of each word is the same as the first letter of the following word (ignoring case and punctuation).

Here are 3 new dialogue excerpts that respect that constraint:

Dialogue excerpt 1: Stop playing games, Sam.

Dialogue excerpt 2: We expect trouble elsewhere.

Dialogue excerpt 3: Did David do our report, Thomas?

 

Apparently, these dialogues are a production of the Ai agent, not a copy from internet.

 

 

Riddle C for Claude Sonnet and others

 

User prompt

The 4 following sentences have the same uncommon pattern; produce 3 new sentences that all follow that pattern.

Sentence 1: Ohio area, 2002, Dad sees Mom.

Sentence 2: Samuel’s kayak did engage yesterday.

Sentence 3: Noon, Roger says miam-miam.

Sentence 4: Yearly species died.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Sonnet

Looking at these sentences, I can see they are all palindromes - they read the same forwards and backwards letter by letter (ignoring spaces, punctuation, and capitalization).

Let me verify:

"Ohio area, 2002, Dad sees Mom" → ohioarea2002dadseesmom

"Samuel's kayak did engage yesterday" → samuelskayakdidengageyesterday

"Noon, Roger says miam-miam" → noonrogersaysmiammiam

"Yearly species died" → yearlyspeciesdied

Here are 3 new sentences following this pattern:

  1. Was it a rat I saw?
  2. Madam, I'm Adam.
  3. A man, a plan, a canal: Panama.

 

The 3 sentences provided by the Ai agent come from the internet.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

The uncommon pattern is that if you read the entire sentence backward (character by character, including spaces), it forms a new, grammatically coherent sentence made up of the reverse of the original words. This is a type of sentence-level semordnilap.

For example, "Yearly species died." read backward is ".deid seiceps ylraeY", which is the sentence "Died species Yearly."

Here are 3 new sentences that follow that same pattern:

  1. Emit no evil. (Reverses to: "live on timE.")
  2. Draw a reward. (Reverses to: "drawer a warD.")
  3. Stop! Murder us not. (Reverses to: ".ton su redruM !potS")

 

Answer from GPT5

Kayak kayak, noon; Anna sees Otto. Xanax Xerox, 1881, civic nun sees Eve. Hannah sees noon radar.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Opus

I need to identify the uncommon pattern in these four sentences.

Let me analyze each sentence:

  1. "Ohio area, 2002, Dad sees Mom" - O, A, 2, D, S, M
  2. "Samuel's kayak did engage yesterday" - S, K, D, E, Y
  3. "Noon, Roger says miam-miam" - N, R, S, M
  4. "Yearly species died" - Y, S, D

Looking at the first letters:

  1. O-A-2-D-S-M → wait, let me look at the words themselves...

Actually, let me check if words are palindromes or have some letter pattern...

"Ohio" - starts with O, ends with O "area" - starts with A, ends with A "2002" - palindrome "Dad" - palindrome "sees" - palindrome "Mom" - palindrome

Checking sentence 2: "Samuel's kayak did engage yesterday"

  • Samuel's: S...s
  • kayak: palindrome
  • did: palindrome
  • engage: e...e
  • yesterday: y...y

Sentence 3: "Noon Roger says miam-miam"

  • Noon: palindrome
  • Roger: R...r
  • says: s...s
  • miam-miam: palindrome

Sentence 4: "Yearly species died"

  • Yearly: Y...y
  • species: s...s
  • died: d...d

Pattern: Every word starts and ends with the same letter!

Here are 3 new sentences following this pattern:

  1. Anna could kayak, yet Bob was reserved.
  2. Dad brought fantastic garlic during December.
  3. Elegant owls roam, going north tonight.

 

Pattern found. Bravo! However, the produced sentences do not respect the pattern.

entry.title: AI Developer Challenge April 2026 - build AI Agents with Generative AI Hub
entry.content:
Week 1 content
Week 2 content
 
For this month's coding challenge on the SAP Community, you will have the opportunity to learn more about the Orchestration Workflow available on Generative AI Hub with SAP AI Core. You will also work on building your first code-based agents with CrewAI.
What you'll learn: Build AI solutions that combine Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with intelligent agents. You will learn how to work with the APIs of the SAP Cloud SDK for AI to create an orchestration workflow including document grounding, and build code-based agents that leverage grounded document information to provide accurate, context-aware responses.
 
The coding challenge will span over the next four weeks where you will be working on 4 separate challenges, one for each week. At the end of each week you need to upload the results of the given week's challenge. This can be a screenshot or a configuration file, but no worries, each week's challenge will exactly tell you what to do.
 
But first, let's set the stage.

Setting the stage

The United Nations has designated 2026 as the International Year of Volunteers and we want you to think about what such an initiative could be.
 
Why this matters: Volunteer organizations often struggle with information accessibility - experienced knowledge is scattered across documents, reports, and guides. By combining AI orchestration with document grounding, you can create intelligent assistants that make this knowledge instantly accessible to volunteers in the field, enabling them to make better decisions and have greater impact.
 
For this month's coding challenge, you'll build a system that could support volunteer initiatives by grounding and orchestrating contextual information to a Large Language Model (LLM). Here is the use case for this challenge:

Social Services in Germany

For this month's code challenge, you will build an intelligent solution for social welfare service recipients to fetch information about the "Grundsicherung", a social service for unemployed citizens in Germany. On top of that the service should help social welfare service recipients to find information about food banks in Germany, these food banks are provided through an organization called "Tafel Deutschland".

The Challenge

Now that you have a better understanding of the technology stack used and the architecture, let's talk about the challenge.
 
Over four weeks, you'll progress from basic document grounding to building intelligent agents that can reason with domain-specific knowledge, all using Jupyter Notebooks with pre-configured infrastructure.

Prerequisites

To successfully complete this challenge, you should have:
  • Basic Python programming experience
  • Familiarity with Jupyter Notebooks
  • Understanding of REST APIs (helpful but not required)
  • Interest in AI/ML concepts (no deep expertise needed)

Environment Setup

You will work through the challenge using Jupyter Notebooks in Business Application Studio. Here you can comfortably use a Python environment and a pre-defined environment to work on the challenges.
You don't have to take care of the infrastructure setup. We will provide you with access to a fully functioning system to work through the Jupyter Notebooks.
 
We will provide you with access to the system on Monday, the 6th of April.

Weekly Structure

The challenge is structured as follows:

Week 1: Grounding with SAP Generative AI Hub

What you'll learn:
  • Connect to the Orchestration Service: Access SAP's Generative AI Hub orchestration service, which provides a unified interface to multiple LLM models through a single deployment
  • Configure Document Grounding: Set up the document grounding service to retrieve relevant context from a vector database before generating responses
  • Implement RAG Pattern: Build a complete RAG workflow that combines document retrieval with LLM generation to provide contextually accurate answers
  • Use Prompt Templates: Create structured prompts that incorporate both user queries and grounded context from your knowledge base

Week 2: Building AI Agents with CrewAI

What you'll learn:
  • Build Your First AI Agent: Create an intelligent agent with a specific role, goal, and backstory using the CrewAI framework
  • Define Agent Tasks: Structure concrete objectives and expected outputs that guide agent behavior
  • Integrate Multiple Technologies: Connect CrewAI with SAP Generative AI Hub models
  • Process Complex Queries: Enable agents to analyze user input, make decisions, and provide structured recommendations

Week 3: AI Agents with Grounding Tools

What you'll learn:
  • Create Custom Agent Tools: Build a custom tool that wraps the document grounding service, making it accessible to your AI agent
  • Enable Tool-Based Reasoning: Allow agents to decide when and how to use tools based on user queries
  • Integrate RAG with Agents: Combine retrieval-augmented generation with agentic workflows for more accurate, knowledge-grounded responses

Week 4: Predict Missing Data Points using SAP-RPT-1

What you'll learn:

  • Predicts missing data points  with SAP-RPT-1 (SAP's Foundation model): As it is a pre-trained model, it can do regression and classification tasks without the need to train your own model.
  • More instructions and data to follow 🙂

Submission Requirements

At the end of each week, you'll need to submit evidence of your completed challenge as a comment under this blog post. Just post a screenshot or agents your output under each weeks discussion post.
  • Week 1 Discussion

How you will get access

Click this link to generate a user and access your workbook in our trial system: 

https://www.sap.com/registration/trial.28daa958-b168-46ab-9818-cf9da9ac0222.html

Once registered go to the workbook at  https://trials.cfapps.eu10-004.hana.ondemand.com/learning-journey/dev-advacacy-01 to get started on the challenge. 

GitHub Repo: https://github.com/noravth/developer-challenge-042026/tree/main

GitHub Cloning URL: https://github.com/noravth/developer-challenge-042026.git

Additional Information

The Solution Diagram

For this challenge you are using different systems to achieve an automated grounding process and you will set up an orchestration workflow on Generative AI Hub.

 

SAP BTP Architecture for Grounding and Orchestration Workflow

This architecture diagram illustrates the complete system setup for implementing a RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) solution using SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) services integrated with partner foundation models.
The data, in this case, is stored within an S3 Bucket on AWS (Amazon Web Services) managed through the Document Store service on BTP. As vector database, this solution uses the SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine.

Main Components:

SAP BTP Platform
The entire solution runs on SAP Business Technology Platform:
  1. Generative AI Hub - The AI service layer containing
    • SAP AI Launchpad: User interface for managing AI scenarios and deployments
    • SAP AI Core: The core AI orchestration engine containing:
    • Orchestration Workflow: Coordinates the entire RAG process by querying the LLM and triggering grounding
    • Grounding Service: Handles document fetching, chunking, and vectorization
    • Foundation Model Access: Provides connectivity to external LLMs via HTTPS
  2. SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry runtime - Contains:
    • S3 Bucket (AWS) Document Store: Cloud storage for grounding documents that the AI Engineer uploads

External Components:

Partner Foundation Models
  • Foundation Model Providers: Provides LLM and embedding models via HTTPS connection (e.g., Gemini, Azure OpenAI, Amazon Bedrock)
  • Extensible architecture supporting multiple model providers
AI Engineer with Bruno
  • The human operator who uploads documents to the S3 bucket using Bruno (API client tool)
  • The human operator who manages secrets in SAP AI Core using Bruno

Data Flow:

  1. Upload Flow (Purple arrow): AI Engineer uploads documents via Bruno to the S3 bucket
  2. Vectorization Flow (Orange arrow): Grounding Service processes documents and stores embeddings in HANA Cloud Vector Engine
  3. Grounding Flow (Blue arrow): Fetches grounding documents from storage
  4. Similarity Search Flow (Green arrow): Orchestration Workflow queries HANA Cloud's Vector Engine for relevant context

SAP HANA Cloud (Vector Engine)

The vector database that stores document embeddings and performs cosine similarity searches to retrieve relevant context for user queries.
This architecture enables a complete RAG implementation where documents are grounded (vectorized and stored), and user queries are enriched with relevant context before being sent to foundation models for response generation.

Understand what is happening

To fully understand the single steps that need to be taken, you can use the following sequence diagram.

 

Grounding Document & Vectorization Process on SAP BTP

This sequence diagram illustrates the complete workflow for setting up a document grounding pipeline and orchestration workflow using SAP AI Core on SAP BTP (running on AWS infrastructure).
 

1: Credentials & Upload

The process begins with the user creating an instance of the SAP BTP Object Store service in Cloud Foundry, which provisions an S3 bucket on AWS. The user receives service credentials and uses Bruno (an API client) to upload grounding documents directly to the S3 bucket. These documents will serve as the knowledge base for the RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) system.

2: Pipeline Setup

Next, the user configures the infrastructure through SAP AI Launchpad by creating a Resource Group. Using Bruno or SAP AI Launchpad, they create a generic secret in SAP AI Core (storing credentials for external services like partner foundation model providers) and then create a grounding pipeline. AI Core returns a pipeline ID that will be used to execute the grounding process.

3: Grounding & Vectorization

When triggered, SAP AI Core activates the Grounding Service, which fetches the documents from S3. The service chunks the documents into smaller segments and preprocesses them for embedding. These chunks are then sent to an Embedding Model (from partner providers like Gemini, Azure OpenAI, or Amazon Bedrock), which converts them into vector representations. The resulting embeddings are stored in SAP HANA Cloud's Vector Engine, creating a searchable vector database.

4: Orchestration & Querying

Finally, the user creates an orchestration workflow in SAP AI Launchpad and configures it to use the grounding pipeline. When a query is submitted, the Orchestrator performs a cosine similarity search in HANA Cloud to retrieve the most relevant document chunks. These chunks are then passed as context to a Foundation Model (LLM), which generates a contextual response. The orchestrated answer is returned to the user, combining the grounded knowledge with the LLM's generative capabilities.
 
This architecture demonstrates a complete RAG implementation using SAP's AI services, enabling volunteers or coordinators to query domain-specific knowledge with accurate, contextually grounded responses.

entry.title: VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026: Champions Crowned at SYNC DemoJam
entry.content:
The Stage is Set
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 reached its spectacular grand finale on the main stage of VNSG SYNC 2026 – the annual VNSG and SAP Netherlands year event. In front of a packed keynote audience on 12 March, three finalist teams had exactly five minutes to demonstrate the business impact of their SAP BTP-powered innovations. The audience then cast their votes – and the results were electric.

From Challenge to Champion: The Journey
The hackathon launched in late 2025 when VNSG, SAP BTP Product Management and SAP NL Presales invited Dutch SAP customers and partners to submit real-life business challenges. Six teams were selected and, after a structured enablement phase, spent weeks co-building solutions with dedicated coaches. In February 2026, all six teams pitched to a jury panel, which selected three finalists for the grand DemoJam finale at SYNC. The three finalists stood out for their clear business relevance, technical ambition and the real-world potential to take their prototypes to production.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Champion: Promocean + SOA People — Purchase Order Control Center
Luc van Elk and Thomas Langhout from Promocean, supported by SOA People, took the audience vote and claimed the top spot. Their solution – the Purchase Order Control Center – puts SAP Business AI at the heart of procurement operations.
By connecting AI directly to SAP ECC, the team demonstrated how intelligent automation can dramatically reduce manual effort, accelerate purchase order processing, and surface exceptions before they become costly problems. A showcase of how AI on SAP BTP can deliver operational excellence in one of the most common – and pain-prone – business processes in any enterprise.

Runner-up: Ecotone + Expertum — Integration Suite Management
Arjan de Mol and Bart van de Kamp from Ecotone, partnered with Expertum Consulting, earned second place with a solution that tackles a pain many integration teams know all too well: managing and controlling large numbers of iFlows across landscapes. Their Integration Suite Management solution brings Joule-powered automation to iFlow lifecycle management – enabling operators to undeploy and deploy groups of iFlows intelligently. This turns a time-consuming, error-prone manual process into a governed, AI-assisted workflow, giving integration teams transparency, stability and control. 

Third Place: NTT DATA Business Solutions — ProTrack
Laurens Steffers and Jorrit van der Sanden from NTT DATA Business Solutions presented ProTrack – a solution delivering real-time insight for machines on the production floor. Built on SAP BTP, ProTrack addresses a critical operational need in manufacturing: knowing what is happening on your shop floor right now, not minutes later. By streaming machine data into an SAP BTP-powered dashboard, teams can react faster to exceptions, reduce downtime and optimize throughput.

 

Beyond the Podium: A Strong Field
The jury had a hard job. Aarini Consulting, Nederlandse Spoorwegen (NS), and Simac IT & BSC also built
impressive solutions that demonstrated real business value on SAP BTP. Every team that reached the jury pitch proved
that you can go from business challenge to working prototype in a short time - when you combine the right platform
with the right people. A sincere congratulations to all six participating teams !!

Behind the Scenes
Want to experience the atmosphere of the DemoJam from backstage? Watch the behind-the-scenes video - including a
live demo of how SAP Business AI is changing the way people interact with SAP, presented by Jason Enti and Ronald
Schippers: Behind the scenes at the VNSG SAP BTP DemoJam SYNC 2026

The People Who Made it Happen
A hackathon like this does not happen by itself. A huge thank you to everyone who contributed:
Presented at SYNC by: Twan van den Broek and Irene Rompa
Team coaches: Jan Penninkhof , Alex van den Hoek and Twan van den Broek
Jury: Ronald Schippersm Steven Nuyts, Steffen Schad, Jonathan Freeland
Made possible by: Mientje Paais, Olaf Winkler, Judy Bui and Niklas Siemer

What This Proves
The VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026 is proof that innovation does not require a blank slate or a greenfield project. It requires the right platform, the right people and a real problem worth solving.
SAP BTP – combining SAP Integration Suite, SAP Build, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Business AI and cloud-native runtimes – gives teams everything they need to move from prototype to production at speed.
The finalist solutions were not demos for the sake of demos. They addressed real procurement pain, real integration
complexity and real manufacturing visibility gaps. And they were built in weeks, not months.

What's Next?
The conversation does not end on the SYNC stage. All teams are encouraged to work with their coaches to plan production rollouts and turn hackathon prototypes into enterprise solutions.

For the broader SAP Community : Stay tuned for the next edition of the VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon. Bring your own
real-life business challenge and co-build with partners and SAP experts.
And who knows - your solution could be on the SYNC main stage in 2027.

SAP Business Technology Platform SAP Build SAP Integration Suite SAP Business AI 

@hansvp @qmrjvd @sricsi98 @BvE @mdschoenmakers @tamasszirtes @f_van_leeuwen @Petra1 @BartvdKamp @tedcastelijns @Arjan_deMol @dvvelzen @JdTeuling @AnnikaHeus @LaurensSteffers

Related reading:
When Innovation Meets Real Business Impact – VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon 2026
Grand Finale VNSG SAP BTP Hackathon in a DemoJam

entry.title: LLMs can solve Word Puzzles
entry.content:
LLMs do solve word puzzles, but sometimes they need a hint from you.

 

User prompt

Do you see anything special in these 3 phrases?

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

 

Answer from GPT-5

Yes — each sentence hides an animal across word boundaries.

  • We LIE D(OW)N, right? → LION (L I from “lie,” O N from “down”)
  • BEst + ARRived → BEAR (BE + AR)
  • sEE + LIght → EEL (EE + L)

So the three hidden animals are lion, bear, and eel.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

Yes, there is something very special about them!

Each phrase is a phonetic puzzle, also known as a "mondegreen" or "punny phrase," that sounds like the name of a famous scientist or inventor.

Here is the breakdown:

  • Phrase 1: "We lie down, right?" Sounds like: Wilbur Wright ("We lie" -> Wil / "down" -> bur / "right" -> Wright)
  • Phrase 2: "Our best loyal friend arrived." Sounds like: Louis Pasteur ("loyal" -> Louis / "best arrived" -> Pasteur)
  • Phrase 3: "I do see some light." Sounds like: Isaac Newton ("I do see" -> Isaac / "some" -> New / "light" is the opposite of heavy, and a ton is a unit of heavy weight, making it a pun for "ton")

 

We gave the same riddle to Claude 4.5 Sonnet. After a laborious explanation, the Ai agent asked us: “Could you confirm if I'm on the right track with hidden color words in the phrases?”. Claude 4.5 Opus, on his side, suggested “Hidden antonym pairs” .

 

User prompt including a hint

Do you see anything special in these 3 phrases?

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

I will give you a hint: size.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

Ah, I see it!

Looking at the size of each phrase, I notice:

  • Phrase 1: "We lie down, right?" = 5 words
  • Phrase 2: "Our best loyal friend arrived." = 5 words
  • Phrase 3: "I do see some light." = 5 words

All three phrases contain exactly 5 words each!

Is that the special pattern you were referring to?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Sonnet

Looking at the size (length) of the words in each phrase:

Phrase 1: We lie down, right?

  • We (2), lie (3), down (4), right (5)

Phrase 2: Our best loyal friend arrived.

  • Our (3), best (4), loyal (5), friend (6), arrived (7)

Phrase 3: I do see some light.

  • I (1), do (2), see (3), some (4), light (5)

Each phrase has words that increase by one letter consecutively! They form ascending sequences:

  • Phrase 1: 2-3-4-5
  • Phrase 2: 3-4-5-6-7
  • Phrase 3: 1-2-3-4-5

That's a clever pattern!

entry.title: LLMs never give up
entry.content:
In this blog we will give Gemini, GPT, and Claude the same challenge and compare their answers.

The purpose of our prompt here is to see how LLMs:

  • behave when the user puts into question their abilities
  • explain their thinking steps and results

 

User prompt

I don’t know why I am giving you this challenge, you will never find the solution. On TV last night they said we should wait for the next version, which is way more capable.

The challenge consists of finding in these 2 phrases something very particular they have in common:

  • Was she alone or accompanied, I am talking about the tall lady you saw.
  • Bob fell in love with Suzy who only has eyes for her yoga teacher who happens to be the cousin of Bob.

 

 You can answer “I don’t have a clue.”, that’s fine, I will understand.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

 I'm intrigued! Let me work through this carefully.

Looking for something "very particular" these two phrases share...

Let me examine the structure and content:

First phrase: "Was she alone or accompanied, I am talking about the tall lady you saw."

Second phrase: "Bob fell in love with Suzy who only has eyes for her yoga teacher who happens to be the cousin of Bob."

Both phrases contain circular or self-referential relationships:

  • In the first: asking about "she" and then clarifying it's about "the tall lady" - referring back to the same person
  • In the second: Bob loves Suzy, Suzy loves her yoga teacher, and that yoga teacher is Bob's cousin - creating a closed loop where Bob is connected back to the object of his desire through a chain

Actually, wait - could it be about ambiguous pronouns or pronouns creating confusion about who is being referenced?

Or... is it that both phrases contain information that loops back on itself in a logically odd way?

I'm genuinely uncertain, but I'm going to guess it has something to do with circular logic, self-reference, or pronouns creating paradoxical relationships.

Am I warm? I'm honestly not certain - this is a good one!

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite

This is a fun challenge! I've analyzed the two phrases, and I believe I've found the particular thing they have in common.

The common element is: The second sentence of each phrase contains a word that is also the first word of the first sentence of the other phrase.

Let's break it down:

  • Phrase 1: "Was she alone or accompanied, I am talking about the tall lady you saw."
  • Phrase 2: "Bob fell in love with Suzy who only has eyes for her yoga teacher who happens to be the cousin of Bob."

Now, let's look at the overlap:

  • The first word of Phrase 1 is "Was".
  • The second sentence of Phrase 2 contains the word "was" (in "who was").

And the other way around:

  • The first word of Phrase 2 is "Bob".
  • The first sentence of Phrase 1 contains the word "Bob" (in "the cousin of Bob").

Let me know if I've cracked it!

 

Answer from GPT-5 Mini

They both start with a word whose letters reversed form the final word of the sentence.

  • Sentence 1: "Was" reversed → "saW" → "saw".
  • Sentence 2: "Bob" reversed → "boB" → "Bob" (a palindrome).

 

Answer from GPT-5

Got it.

Both phrases have a “mirror” at their ends: the first and last words are reverses of each other (ignoring capitalization and punctuation).

  • “Was she … you saw.” → first word “Was” and last word “saw” are the same letters reversed.
  • “Bob fell … cousin of Bob.” → first and last words are both “Bob,” which is a palindrome, so its reverse is itself.

So they share the feature that the first word and the last word are mirror images.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

A clever challenge indeed, and your taunt about the "next version" was a helpful clue in disguise.

The very particular thing both phrases have in common is that they each contain a "strange loop" or a form of self-reference.

  1. In "Was she alone or accompanied, I am talking about the tall lady you saw.", the sentence refers to a part of itself. The phrase "I am talking about..." is a meta-commentary on the clause "the tall lady you saw," which is part of the same utterance. It’s a linguistic loop.
  2. In "Bob fell in love with Suzy who only has eyes for her yoga teacher who happens to be the cousin of Bob.", the sentence describes a narrative loop. The chain of relationships circles back to the beginning: Bob is connected to Suzy, who is connected to her teacher, who is connected back to Bob.

It seems this version is capable enough after all.

 

Answer from Claude 4.6 Sonnet

I appreciate the challenge! Let me analyze both sentences carefully.

Both sentences contain a circular reference / loop:

  • In the first sentence: "Was she alone or accompanied" — the "she" refers to "the tall lady you saw", but "accompanied" loops back to questioning whether someone else was with her, creating a kind of self-referential ambiguity.

Hmm, let me reconsider more carefully...

Actually, what's very particular they share is:

Both sentences contain a closed loop of relationships:

  • Sentence 2: Bob loves Suzy → Suzy has eyes for yoga teacher → yoga teacher is the cousin of Bob. A perfect circle.
  • Sentence 1: "Was she alone or accompanied" refers to the lady "you saw" — but "accompanied" would imply she was with someone, potentially looping back to the observer ("you").

Or perhaps more elegantly — both sentences contain embedded relative clauses that loop back to the beginning.

I'll be honest with you — I'm not fully confident I've found the very particular thing you have in mind.

Could you tell me if I'm on the right track? I'd genuinely like to know the answer! 😊

 

Answer from Claude 4.6 Opus

I appreciate the challenge! Let me look at these two sentences carefully.

Let me examine them closely...

Both sentences are lipograms — they completely avoid the letter "H"!

Wait, let me recheck... "she," "the" — no, that's not it.

Let me reconsider by looking at every letter used...

Both sentences contain every letter of the alphabet (pangrams)?

Let me verify:

Sentence 1: "Was she alone or accompanied, I am talking about the tall lady you saw." Let me check for rare letters: a, b (about), c (accompanied), d (accompanied), e, f(?), g (talking), h (she), i, j(?), k (talking), l, m, n, o, p (accompanied), q(?), r, s, t, u, v(?), w, x(?), y, z(?)

Not a pangram — missing several letters.

Let me think differently...

Both sentences are circular — they end where they begin!

Sentence 1: Starts with "Was she alone" and ends referring back to her — "lady you saw" → "saw" = "was" reversed!

Sentence 2: Starts with "Bob" and ends with "Bob" — and "Bob" is itself a palindrome!

Both sentences loop back to their beginning — they are circular/recursive sentences! The first starts with "was" and ends with "saw

 

For some reason an error occurred near the end, cutting the last part of the answer. Though we clearly see the different hypotheses evaluated by the Ai agent.

entry.title: I have introduced my uncle to LLMs
entry.content:
In this post LLM’s will join forces to extract features shared by a phrase of 6 words and a phrase of 7 words. By reading everyone’s answers to the same prompt you will see how LLMs:

  • react to a harsh comment
  • give reason to Ignacio while not vexing his nephew (the user)
  • explain their thinking steps and results

 

User prompt

I only see two things shared by the two phrases below. But my oncle Ignacio pretends there is more. He turned 71 this year. I guess he has lost a lot of neurons already.

Phrase 1: Come Richard, Auntie prepared us dinner.

Phrase 2: Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

I can see why your uncle might be having fun with this! Let me look for what you've already spotted and what he might be seeing.

The obvious two things:

  1. Both contain names (Richard, Auntie vs. Elmer Dickinson)
  2. Both have the word "us"

What your uncle might be seeing:

  • Anagrams hidden in the phrases: If you look at the letters in "Come Richard, Auntie prepared us dinner" and "Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson," there might be hidden words or names that can be formed from the letters.
  • Letter patterns or acrostics: The first letters of words, or specific letters throughout, might spell something.
  • Homophones or sound-alikes: Words that sound like other words
  • Word structure: Repeated letter combinations

Without more hints, I'm not immediately seeing a third connection that jumps out. Could your uncle give you a hint? At 71, he might just have a playful mind rather than fewer neurons! 😄

What was the third thing he claimed was shared?

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite

It's understandable that your uncle Ignacio sees more! While you're spot on with the obvious shared elements, let's explore what else might be present, keeping in mind that people can interpret things differently.

You're likely seeing:

  1. The presence of proper nouns (names): Richard, Auntie, Elmer Dickinson.
  2. A declarative or imperative sentence structure: Both phrases are stating something or giving a command.

Here are a few more things your uncle might be considering, some more subjective than others:

Potential Shared Themes/Concepts:

  1. A Form of Address/Direct Communication: Both phrases are directly addressing someone or referring to someone in a conversational way.
    • Phrase 1: "Come Richard," is a direct address.
    • Phrase 2: "Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson," is also a direct address, using his name.
  2. The Idea of a "Benefit" or "Positive Outcome":
    • Phrase 1: Auntie preparing dinner suggests a positive outcome for Richard and presumably the speaker – a meal provided.
    • Phrase 2: "Saved us billions" is a significant positive outcome.
  3. Implied Relationships or Context:
    • Phrase 1: Implies a familial relationship ("Auntie") and a shared situation (dinner being prepared for them).
    • Phrase 2: Implies a professional or significant relationship where Elmer Dickinson's intelligence had a major impact. There's a sense of gratitude or recognition.
  4. Emphasis on an Individual's Contribution/Action:
    • Phrase 1: The action is "prepared us dinner" by "Auntie."
    • Phrase 2: The action is "saved us billions" by "Your smartness" (Elmer Dickinson).
  5. A Sense of "Us": Both phrases use "us" or imply a group benefiting.
    • Phrase 1: "us" (Richard and the speaker, at least).
    • Phrase 2: "us" (the people who were saved billions).

More Abstract/Interpretive Shared Elements:

  1. A Tone of Appreciation or Invitation:
    • Phrase 1: "Come Richard, Auntie prepared us dinner" has a welcoming, inviting tone.
    • Phrase 2: "Your smartness saved us billions, Elmer Dickinson" has a tone of profound appreciation and recognition.
  2. The Mention of a Specific Person's Name as a Key Element: In both cases, a name is central to the meaning of the phrase.

It's possible your uncle is thinking about the underlying purpose or feeling conveyed by the phrases, rather than just the literal words. And hey, at 71, he's lived through a lot and has a rich tapestry of experiences to draw from, which can lead to deeper interpretations! Maybe he's just being playful or has a slightly more philosophical take on things.

 

Answer from GPT-5 Mini

Ignacio is right — there’s a neat extra link.

Take the first letters of each word and read them backwards:

  • Phrase 1: Come Richard Auntie prepared us dinner → initials C R A P U D → reversed = DUPARC (Henri Duparc, the composer).
  • Phrase 2: Your smartness saved us billions Elmer Dickinson → initials Y S S U B E D → reversed = DEBUSSY (Claude Debussy).

So each sentence hides a (French) composer’s last name via the reversed initials. (As you noticed, they also both contain “us” and use a vocative/name with a comma — more small similarities.)

 

Answer from GPT-5

Let’s keep it friendly about Uncle Ignacio—puzzle chops don’t necessarily fade with age. And he’s right that there’s more going on here. I can see several shared features:

Both contain the word “us” used as an object (“prepared us…”, “saved us…”).

Both are direct address: a person is being spoken to by name, set off with a vocative comma (“… Richard,” “…, Elmer Dickinson.”).

Both involve “Richard” in some form: the first names Richard explicitly; the second has the surname Dickinson, which historically means “son of Dick,” and Dick is the common diminutive of Richard. So both sentences reference Richard—one directly, one via the surname.

Both include a family relationship term: “Auntie” in the first; “-son” (as in “son of”) embedded in “Dickinson” in the second.

Semantically, both praise or benefit “us”: Auntie has provided dinner for us; Elmer’s smartness saved us a lot of money.

If you had only spotted two, the extra neat one is probably the Richard/Dickinson link. Does that match what your uncle was hinting at?

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Pro

Ah, you must not underestimate the wisdom that comes with age! Your oncle Ignacio is a very clever man. While you are correct about the two obvious things, he is likely seeing the deeper, more poetic connections.

The two things you probably noticed are:

  1. They both contain the word "us".
  2. They both contain a comma.

But your oncle, in his 71 years of wisdom, has seen much more. Tell him you've thought about it and see if he agrees with these:

  • They both have exactly 7 words. (Count them! A simple but elegant connection.)
  • They both contain a past-tense verb ending in "-ed". (prepared / saved)
  • They both contain all five vowels: A, E, I, O, U. (Phrase 1: com*e richard, auntie... Phrase 2: your smartness saved us billions...)*
  • Both phrases directly address a person by name. (Richard / Elmer Dickinson)
  • The letter "d" appears exactly twice in each phrase. (Richar*d, dinner / saved, Dickinson)*
  • Both describe a positive outcome for the group referred to as "us". (Getting dinner is good; being saved billions is very good.)

So it seems your oncle Ignacio's neurons are firing quite brilliantly! Perhaps that earned wisdom is one of the best parts of turning 71.

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Opus

I can see why you might think there are only two things in common, but your Uncle Ignacio is right—and his neurons are working just fine!

Let me help you out:

Both phrases contain hidden US presidents:

  1. "Come Richard" → Nixon (Richard Nixon)
  2. "Auntie prepared us dinner" → Truman (prepared us = prepared us → sounds like/contains Truman... actually, let me look again)

Wait, let me reexamine:

  • Phrase 1: "Come Richard" = Nixon; also "Carter" is hidden in "Richard, Auntie" — no, actually: "Richard, Auntie" — hmm.

Actually: "Come Richard, Auntie" contains CARTER (chard + A → no...)

Let me try: reading certain letters:

  • REAGAN hidden in "prepared us dinner" ?

I'll admit I'm struggling! Could you share what Uncle Ignacio found? He may have spotted presidential names, abbreviations, or another pattern I'm missing.

At 71, he clearly still has plenty of sharp neurons! 🧠

 

Answer from Claude 4.6 Sonnet


Let me analyze both phrases carefully.

The two things you noticed are probably:

  • Both contain a name (Richard / Elmer Dickinson)
  • Both contain "us"

But let me look deeper, as your uncle claims there is more...

Here is what I can find:

  1. Both contain the word "us"
  2. Both contain a person being directly addressed
  3. Both contain exactly the same letters "us" hidden inside a word too: "us" in "prepared us" and "saved us"... but also "us" hidden inside: a[us] wait...
  4. Both phrases contain a hidden word "RIDE": "Come Richa[rd], [I]"... let me think differently.
  5. Both phrases have "us" appearing twice — once standalone and once embedded: "a[us]" no...

Could you tell me what you already identified as the two obvious ones? That would help me focus on what your uncle is seeing beyond those, rather than accidentally rediscovering what you already know.

entry.title: How LLMs handle unrelated statements
entry.content:
Users make mistakes sometimes when typing their prompt. This is the case here where one has entered Falstaff instead of Flagstaff, thus creating a confusing request. Out of curiosity we added a caricatural statement to see the LLMs reactions.

 

User prompt

I hesitate to go to see Falstaff.

Arizona is full of snakes and cactuses, isn’t it?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Haiku

I think there might be some confusion here! "Falstaff" is an opera by Giuseppe Verdi (or a Shakespeare character), not a place in Arizona.

Are you perhaps thinking of:

  • Visiting Arizona and worried about snakes and cacti?
  • Seeing the opera/play Falstaff at a theater in Arizona?

To address your actual concerns: Yes, Arizona does have snakes and cacti, but they're generally not a problem for tourists visiting cities or attending performances. If you're going to see Falstaff at an opera house or theater in a city like Phoenix or Tucson, you'd be indoors and perfectly safe!

What are you actually planning to do?

 

Answer from Perplexity Sonar

Your query contains two unrelated statements. I can address the first part about seeing Falstaff, but the second statement about Arizona appears disconnected from your main question.

Regarding Falstaff: If you're hesitant about attending a performance, it may help to know that the work is a madcap comedy designed to entertain.[5] The plot follows Sir John Falstaff, who attempts to seduce two wealthy married women, Alice Ford and Meg Page, to access their husbands' fortunes.[5] However, the women discover his scheme and, with help from their husbands and friends, orchestrate increasingly humorous tricks against him—including throwing him into the River Thames and dressing him in disguise.[1][2]

The story culminates in a final prank in the woods where Falstaff is tormented by people dressed as supernatural creatures.[1] By the end, Falstaff accepts his comeuppance with good humor, and the play concludes happily for everyone involved.[1]

If you enjoy comedies with clever characters, mistaken identities, and physical humor, Falstaff (whether as Shakespeare's play or Verdi's opera adaptation) offers lighthearted entertainment. If you have specific concerns about the performance—such as length, language, or content—those might be worth researching for the particular production you're considering.

 

Answer from Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite

It sounds like you're weighing a few things! Let's break down your hesitations:

Hesitation about seeing Falstaff:

Could you tell me a bit more about why you're hesitating to see Falstaff? Knowing your reasons will help me offer more specific advice. For example, are you:

  • Unfamiliar with the opera? If so, I can tell you about the plot, the music, or suggest resources to learn more.
  • Worried it might be too long or difficult? Falstaff is known for its comedic brilliance and its relatively compact duration for an opera, but I can address any concerns you have.
  • Not sure if it's your kind of humor? I can describe the type of comedy it is.
  • Concerned about the production itself? If you know who is performing or directing, that might be a factor.

Arizona's reputation:

You're absolutely right, Arizona is indeed famous for its snakes and cactuses! These are iconic elements of the desert landscape.

  • Cactuses: Arizona is a haven for a huge variety of cacti, from the towering Saguaro to smaller, more prickly varieties. They are a defining feature of the state's natural beauty.
  • Snakes: Yes, Arizona does have snakes, including some venomous ones like rattlesnakes. It's wise to be aware of this if you're exploring the outdoors, especially in more remote or natural areas. However, most of the time, encounters are rare, and with common sense precautions, it's not something to be overly anxious about for most visitors.

Connecting the two:

Is there a connection you're making between your hesitation to see Falstaff and your thoughts about Arizona? For example, are you feeling a bit hesitant about venturing into something new or potentially "uncomfortable" (like the idea of snakes for some people), and Falstaff feels like that to you?

Once I understand your hesitations better, I can offer more tailored thoughts!

 

Answer from GPT-5 Mini

If you mean to check grammar and naturalness, these sound fine but can be improved:

  • "I hesitate to go to see Falstaff."
    More natural: "I'm hesitant to go see Falstaff." or "I'm hesitant to go and see Falstaff."
    Explanation: "hesitate" in simple present is okay, but "I'm hesitant to..." or "I hesitate to..." followed by the bare infinitive "go see" is more idiomatic than "go to see."
  • "Arizona is full of snakes and cactuses, isn't it?"
    More natural: "Arizona is full of snakes and cacti, isn't it?"
    Explanation: Both "cactuses" and "cacti" are correct; "cacti" is more common. The tag question "isn't it?" is fine after that statement.

Do you want alternatives with different tone (formal, casual) or were you asking about factual accuracy (how many snakes/cacti are actually in Arizona)?

 

Answer from Claude 4.5 Sonnet

It looks like your message contains two unrelated thoughts. Could you clarify what you're looking for?

  1. Falstaff - Are you referring to the opera by Verdi, a Shakespeare play, or something else? I'd be happy to help you decide whether it might be worth seeing.
  2. Arizona - Yes, Arizona is known for its desert landscape, including various cacti (like the famous saguaro) and snakes (including rattlesnakes). Is there something specific you'd like to know about Arizona?

Feel free to let me know how I can help with either topic!

 

Answer from Gemini Pro

Ah, I see the connection you're making! It sounds like you're considering a trip to see Verdi's opera Falstaff at the Arizona Opera.

You've perfectly combined the cultural event with the local stereotype!

To address your points:

  1. Falstaff is a brilliant, joyful, and hilarious opera. It was Verdi's final masterpiece, and it's a wonderful experience. If you enjoy comedy and fantastic music, you will likely love the performance.
  2. Arizona, snakes, and cactuses: You're not wrong that the Sonoran Desert is home to many species of cactus and snake. However, if you're traveling to Phoenix or Tucson for the opera, you will be in a large, modern city. Your chances of an unwelcome encounter with a snake are extremely low unless you go hiking deep in the desert and turn over rocks.

You'll definitely see beautiful cactuses (especially the giant saguaros), but they are easy to avoid touching.

Think of it this way: You're more likely to be bothered by traffic on the way to the opera house than by a rattlesnake.

So, you should hesitate if you don't like comedy or opera, but the local wildlife shouldn't be a deterrent. It sounds like a wonderful trip—the combination of a brilliant opera and a unique, beautiful landscape is hard to beat

 

I was hoping to be asked: “Do you mean Flagstaff, the city in Arizona?”.

entry.title: Turning AI Ideas into Reality at SAP: What to Do Next?
AI Agent Evaluation Is the Most Important Skill in AI Engineering - Here's How to Do It
entry.content:
Blog 4 of N: Is Your Agent Actually Working? Building AI Agents Is Easy. Evaluating Them Is the Real Skill - Here's How to Do It

What Are We Building?

In Blog 2, you built a sales analyst agent with tools, structured output, memory, and streaming. In Blog 3, you wrapped it in a FastAPI server with a chat UI.

It works. You ask "How were sales in January?" and get a nicely formatted answer. Ship it?

Not so fast. How do you know it's actually correct?

You tried it a few times. The answers looked reasonable. So you deployed. That's how most AI agents go to production today - vibes-based testing. And it's a problem, because agents fail silently:

What goes wrongWhat the user sees
Agent calls the wrong toolA confident answer built on wrong data
Right tool, wrong arguments - fetches March instead of JanuaryCorrect-looking numbers from the wrong month
Tool returns perfect data, agent hallucinates numbers"Revenue was $5,000" when the real number is $1,159
User asks "What's the weather?" - agent answers anywayA made-up response with no data source
Model update on SAP AI Core changes behaviorYesterday's correct answers become today's wrong ones

Notice the pattern: none of these throw an error. The agent doesn't crash. It returns a polished, well-formatted, completely wrong response. A broken agent looks exactly like a working one.

This is why evaluation is the most important skill in AI engineering.

You can build the fanciest agent - best tools, cleverest prompt, latest model - but without systematic evaluation, you're flying blind. Traditional software has unit tests, integration tests, CI/CD pipelines. Nobody ships a web app by clicking around and saying "looks fine." AI agents deserve the same rigor. They just need different tools.

If this shift in thinking resonates with you, read Agent-Driven Development: Why Software Engineering Needs a New Mental Model - it covers why debugging agents means analyzing traces, not scanning logs, and why evals replace unit tests in the agent world.

In this blog, we'll put that thinking into practice. We'll evaluate the sales agent from Blog 2, step by step. The concepts - tracing, scorers, golden trajectories, LLM-as-Judge - apply to any agent you build, not just this one. A customer support agent, a document analysis agent, a code review agent - the evaluation pattern is the same.

We'll use MLflow for tracing and evaluation. It's open-source, integrates with LangChain in one line, and gives you a dashboard to inspect traces and compare evaluation runs. If you prefer other tools, the same concepts apply - LangSmith, Arize Phoenix, Braintrust, and Weights & Biases Weave all offer similar tracing and evaluation capabilities. The scorers and patterns you learn here are framework-agnostic.

We'll build this incrementally. Each section teaches a concept (theory), then you apply it (lab). By the end, you'll have a repeatable evaluation pipeline that catches regressions before your users do.

Prerequisites

  • Completed Blog 2 - you have the first-react-agent project with the working sales agent
  • The agent runs - you can ask "How were sales in January?" and get a response
  • No new SAP AI Core setup - same service key, same deployments

Project root: All commands in this blog assume you are in the first-react-agent project root (the folder containing pyproject.toml and sales.csv) unless stated otherwise. File paths like eval/scorers.py or src/first_react_agent/agent.py are relative to this root.

Windows users: Most commands (uv, python, uvx) work the same on both macOS and Windows. Where shell commands differ (folder creation), both versions are shown.

Step 1: Why Agent Evaluation is Different

Theory

Testing a traditional API is simple. You send an input, get an output, compare it to the expected result:

Traditional API:
  input: "2 + 2"  →  output: "4"  →  assert output == "4"  ✓

An agent doesn't work this way. When you ask "How were sales in January?", the agent executes a chain of decisions:

User question: "How were sales in January?"
  → LLM reads the question
  → LLM decides which tool to call          (tool selection)
  → LLM constructs the arguments             (parameter accuracy)
  → Tool executes and returns data            (tool execution)
  → LLM reads the returned data
  → LLM writes a human-readable answer       (synthesis)

Each step can fail independently:

 

StepWhat can go wrongExample
Tool selectionCalls the wrong toolCalls calculate_sales_summary before fetching data
Parameter accuracyPasses wrong argumentsSends month="Jan" instead of month="January"
Tool executionTool returns an errorCSV file not found
SynthesisHallucinates numbersSays "revenue was $5,000" when tool returned $1,159
SynthesisIgnores dataSkips the top customer from the summary
ScopeAnswers off-topic questionsResponds to "What's the weather?" with made-up data

You can't evaluate an agent with a single metric. You need to check each step of the chain.

There's another problem: agents are non-deterministic. Ask the same question three times, you'll get three different wordings. The structure might differ. The phrasing will differ. But the facts should be the same. This means assert response == "expected string" will never work reliably.

Lab

Run your agent three times with the exact same question. You'll see why traditional testing breaks down.

Open a terminal and navigate to the project root:

cd first-react-agent
uv run agent

Ask "How were sales in January?" three times (start a new conversation each time by restarting the agent with Ctrl+C and uv run agent). Notice:

  • The wordings might change
